title: Vital Documents Access for Unaccompanied Homeless Youth Act of 2025
short_title: Vital Documents Access for Unaccompanied Homeless Youth Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill sets up a special group of government workers and people who help homeless youth to work together on an important problem. Many homeless young people cannot get basic identity papers like birth certificates and social security cards that they need for jobs, school, and housing. The group will meet regularly to share ideas that work and come up with new ways to help these youth get their important documents. They will then write reports to Congress to tell them what they learned and what changes should be made to better help homeless young people.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Vital Documents Access for Unaccompanied Homeless Youth Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Establishment of Interagency Task Force on Vital Documents Access for Unaccompanied Homeless Youth; Reports
sections.text:
This section creates a special working group called the Interagency Task Force on Vital Documents Access for Unaccompanied Homeless Youth. Within ninety days of this law being passed, the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development, the Secretary of Health and Human Services, and the Commissioner of Social Security must work together to set up this group. The task force will help coordinate efforts and share the best ways to help homeless youth who are not with parents or guardians get important documents like birth certificates and social security cards.

The task force will have several members. It will include a chairperson, the Commissioner of Social Security, the two secretaries mentioned above, three directors from state human services departments, and three representatives from national nonprofit organizations that help homeless youth. The President will appoint some members, while leaders in the Senate and House of Representatives will appoint others. All the appointed members must be under thirty years old and must have personal experience with youth homelessness.

Members will serve for as long as the task force exists. If someone leaves, a replacement must be chosen within thirty days using the same process that picked the original person. The chairperson will be chosen jointly by the Commissioner of Social Security and the two secretaries. The task force can hire additional staff to help with their work.

Starting at the end of the first three-month period after the task force is created, the group must meet every three months. During these meetings, they will discuss how much progress each member has made in helping homeless youth get vital documents. They will talk about what policies and practices have worked, what challenges they have faced, and how they have improved their approaches. The task force will create a system for collecting information about how well different policies work. They will use this information to develop new policies and evaluate how successful they are. They will also create policies that federal, state, and local agencies can use to improve access to vital documents for homeless youth.

One year after the task force is created, the two secretaries and the Social Security Commissioner must submit an initial report to Congress about how to increase vital document access for homeless youth. This report must include a detailed analysis of the challenges homeless youth face when trying to access government programs without proper documents. It must provide recommendations for new laws and administrative actions to increase access to programs like housing assistance, health care, and social services. The report must list state and local policies that have successfully helped homeless youth get vital documents and recommend ways to expand these policies. The Social Security Commissioner must provide a detailed assessment of policies for giving social security cards to homeless youth, including when documentation requirements can be waived. The Commissioner must also recommend ways to increase social security card access, propose new educational materials to help with applications, create a plan for better outreach and collaboration, and suggest state-level changes.

Three years after the task force is created, it must submit a final report to Congress. This report must describe what changes each member made based on the initial report recommendations and how effective these changes were in helping homeless youth get vital documents. The report must also assess whether the task force should become permanent.

For this section, covered secretaries means the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development and the Secretary of Health and Human Services. Unaccompanied homeless youth means a homeless child or youth as defined in the McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance Act who is not in the physical custody of a parent or guardian. Vital documents means any state or federal document that can be used to establish someone's identity, including social security cards and birth certificates.

The task force will end three years after it is created.
